title: Open One Investment Article and Read It Through
subtitle: An activity for breaking the avoidance pattern around financial learning
excerpt: A single-session activity for the person who has been meaning to learn about investing for months or years. One article, read in full, with no decisions attached. The point is the act of looking, not the outcome.

practice.whenToUse: Use when investing has been on the mental to-do list for a long time and the avoidance has become a habit in itself.

content.text: An activity for breaking the loop of intending to learn about investing and not doing it. The loop is usually maintained by the belief that when you start, you'll need to make a decision, and you're not ready to make a decision. This activity separates the two: you read, and you don't decide.

Find one article on a beginner investing topic — index funds, pensions, ISAs, whatever is most relevant to where you are. A government financial guidance site, a consumer financial publication, a reputable money educator. Nothing requiring a subscription.
Read it through to the end. All of it. Don't skip to the summary.
When you've finished, write down one thing you now understand that you didn't before you started. One sentence is enough.
That's it. No decisions required today. You have read one article on investing. You know one more thing than you did. You can stop there.

Most people who do this activity find that the article was more readable than expected. The avoidance tends to be based on a belief about the difficulty level, which the article itself disproves. The point of doing it is that the belief then has to update.
